_schemaNotes: Phase 2: all 7 Oncology modalities fully authored (concept, schematicParts, pros, cons, verdict, exampleDrugIds). schematicParts are text labels only — accurate illustrated schematics are a Phase 4/5 deliverable, not part of this content pass. Immunology stubs remain name-only per Section 8 of the locked spec.

modalities.name: ADC (Antibody-Drug Conjugate)
modalities.concept: An antibody guides a toxic drug directly to cancer cells via a chemical linker — like a guided missile.
pros: Targeted, spares healthy tissue
Very potent payloads
Proven blockbusters
cons: Complex/costly manufacturing
Off-target toxicity
Resistance

modalities.name: Targeted mAb
modalities.concept: A lab-made antibody that recognizes one specific protein on cancer cells and either blocks its growth signal directly or flags the cell for the immune system to destroy — oncology's original precision therapy, dating to the late 1990s.
pros: Highly selective for tumor cells, spares healthy tissue
Better tolerated than conventional chemotherapy
Can work through multiple mechanisms at once
cons: Only works if the tumor expresses the target antigen
Large molecules can't reach some tumor sites (e.g. brain)
Resistance can develop over time
modalities.verdict: The foundational modality — proven and safe, but often needs a combination partner for full effect.

modalities.name: Bispecific Antibody
modalities.concept: An antibody engineered to grab two different targets at once — a cancer cell on one arm, a T-cell on the other — physically dragging the immune cell in to attack, with no need to engineer the patient's own cells.
pros: Off-the-shelf — no need to harvest/engineer patient cells like CAR-T
Can achieve CAR-T-like response rates in blood cancers
Fast-growing class: multiple approvals since 2014
cons: Cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity risk, similar to CAR-T
Short half-life often requires frequent or continuous infusion
Efficacy in solid tumors still limited
modalities.verdict: A cheaper, more scalable alternative to cell therapy — a fast-growing modality class.

modalities.name: Cell Therapy (CAR-T)
modalities.concept: A patient's own T-cells are extracted, genetically re-engineered to carry a receptor that recognizes a cancer marker, then infused back in as a living drug that can multiply and persist in the body.
pros: Deep, durable remissions in relapsed/refractory blood cancers
Living drug — can persist for years, guarding against relapse
Six approved products since 2017 for hematologic malignancies
cons: Serious toxicities: cytokine release syndrome, neurotoxicity
Very expensive ($300K–$475K per treatment)
Complex, slow, patient-specific manufacturing; limited solid-tumor success so far
modalities.verdict: The most powerful but most operationally demanding modality — reserved for cases nothing else can touch.

modalities.name: Small Molecule (Kinase Inhibitor)
modalities.concept: A small chemical compound taken as a pill that fits into the active site of a mutated enzyme (kinase) driving tumor growth, switching it off — the way a key jams a lock.
pros: Oral dosing — convenient, no infusion needed
High efficacy with relatively mild side effects in the right tumor type
Cheap to manufacture relative to biologics
cons: Resistance mutations commonly emerge over time
Only effective when the tumor is 'addicted' to that specific kinase
Off-target binding can cause unexpected toxicities
modalities.verdict: The workhorse modality — simple to deliver, but resistance is the constant fight.

modalities.name: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor
modalities.concept: An antibody that blocks the 'brakes' cancer cells use to hide from the immune system (like PD-1/PD-L1 or CTLA-4), releasing the immune system to recognize and attack the tumor.
pros: Durable, sometimes years-long responses in a subset of patients
Works across many tumor types, not just one
Response can persist even after stopping treatment
cons: Only a minority of patients respond
Immune-related adverse events can affect any organ
Response patterns can be unpredictable (pseudo-progression before shrinkage)
modalities.verdict: Transformed oncology by unleashing the immune system — but who responds is still hard to predict.

modalities.name: Radioligand Therapy
modalities.concept: A tumor-seeking molecule is chemically bonded to a radioactive isotope; once it locks onto the target on cancer cells, it delivers a localized dose of radiation that damages the cell from within.
pros: Delivers radiation directly to tumor cells, sparing healthy tissue
Proven survival benefit in prostate cancer and neuroendocrine tumors
Pairs with imaging for a 'see it, treat it' theranostic approach
cons: Requires specialized nuclear medicine infrastructure and handling
Global supply and workforce shortages limit access
Still carries meaningful Grade 3+ adverse event rates
modalities.verdict: A precision-radiation modality with real survival data — bottlenecked by infrastructure, not science.
